content: Hey Saturday, I've been thinking about getting a dog for a while now and I'm finally ready to make it happen. The thing is, I live in a small apartment in the city and I'm not sure what breed would be best for my lifestyle. I work from home most days but I do travel for work sometimes. What should I consider?

That's exciting! Living in an apartment with a work-from-home situation actually opens up some good options. The key factors you'll want to think about are: energy level (since you don't have a yard), size constraints, temperament, and how they handle being alone during your travel days. Lower-energy breeds like Bulldogs, Basset Hounds, or even some smaller mixed breeds tend to do really well in apartments. Since you travel occasionally, you might also want a dog that doesn't have severe separation anxiety. Have you thought about whether you prefer a puppy or an adult dog? Puppies need way more training time, but adult shelter dogs often come already housetrained and their personality is already developed.

I think an adult dog makes more sense for my schedule, honestly. I don't have the patience for intensive puppy training. I'm leaning toward either a medium-sized dog or even a small one since space is tight. Are there specific breeds you'd recommend that are good for apartments and don't need like three hours of exercise a day?

Perfect, adult dogs are often overlooked but they're fantastic for people in your situation. For medium-to-small apartment dogs, consider Corgis (surprisingly chill despite being herding dogs), French Bulldogs, Cavalier King Charles Spaniels, or even Beagles if you don't mind some barking. Boston Terriers are another great choice—they're small, affectionate, and genuinely happy with a couple of 20-minute walks a day. If you want something even more low-key, Shih Tzus and Lhasa Apsos are bred to be companion dogs and are content in smaller spaces. The honest truth though is that a lot of mixed breeds at shelters can be perfect matches—sometimes a Shepherd mix or Lab mix that's 2-3 years old will have calmed down significantly from their puppy days and will be exactly what you need. Have you looked into any shelters near you yet?

I haven't started looking yet, but there's a pretty big shelter about 15 minutes from my apartment called the Metropolitan Animal Rescue. I've heard good things about them. Should I just go in and see what they have, or is there a better approach to finding the right dog?

Metropolitan Animal Rescue is a great starting point! I'd recommend calling ahead first to ask about their process and what dogs they currently have that might fit your criteria—it'll save you time and help them suggest matches before you visit. Most shelters have websites with photos and bios of available dogs now, so you can browse beforehand and maybe get a sense of which dogs appeal to you. When you do visit, bring a list of must-haves versus nice-to-haves: apartment-friendly, moderate energy, good with strangers if you have friends over, etc. The staff at good shelters like Metro will spend time talking to you about your lifestyle and won't just adopt you the first cute dog they see. They want the adoption to stick. Also, pro tip: go on a weekday afternoon if you can—it's quieter and the dogs are less stimulated, so you can better assess their real temperament.

That's really helpful. I'll definitely look them up online and call first. One thing I'm worried about though—I've heard that shelter dogs sometimes have behavioral issues or trauma from before. How do I know if a dog has a good temperament or if there might be red flags I should watch out for?

That's a legit concern, but here's the good news: shelter staff are trained to assess temperament and they're usually upfront about any issues. When you meet a dog, watch for things like excessive fearfulness (cowering, not making eye contact), aggression signs (growling, showing teeth, stiff body), or overly manic energy. A dog that's a little shy is often fine—they warm up with time. Ask the staff directly about the dog's history, how it does with other dogs and kids, any medical issues, and how long it's been at the shelter. Reputable shelters do behavioral assessments already. A dog might be cautious on day one because it's stressed in a new environment, but that doesn't mean it has trauma. Most shelter dogs adjust beautifully within a few weeks once they understand their new home is safe. That said, if something feels off or you don't have a connection, it's totally okay to pass and keep looking. The right match will feel right.

Okay that makes me feel better. Let me ask about the practical side now—I know I'll need to buy a bunch of stuff before bringing a dog home. What's the essential equipment I should get, and are there things people waste money on that I should avoid?

Great question because yeah, people definitely overbuy. Here's the essentials: food and water bowls (stainless steel or ceramic are best, avoid plastic which can harbor bacteria), a collar and leash, a bed or crate for their safe space, and age-appropriate food. Since you have an adult dog, you'll get breed-size and dietary recommendations from the shelter or vet. Beyond that, toys are good but you don't need twenty—a mix of textures helps: rope toys, rubber balls, chew toys. A grooming brush appropriate for their coat type is worth it. Avoid: fancy designer beds they'll destroy, those expensive treat dispenser balls if the dog's not toy-motivated, excessive clothes unless they really need them. What people often undersestimate is the vet fund—plan for annual checkups, vaccinations, and an emergency buffer. Maybe set aside $500-1000 for unexpected vet stuff in the first year. What's your budget looking like for initial setup?

I'm thinking maybe $300-400 for initial supplies, and I can handle vet costs. I haven't thought about food costs yet though. How much should I expect to spend monthly on dog food and other recurring expenses like vet visits?

For a small-to-medium dog eating decent quality food, expect $30-60 per month depending on the brand and whether you go premium or mid-range. If you get a larger dog it could creep up to $80-100 monthly. The other recurring costs are: annual vet checkup ($100-200), vaccinations if needed (usually bundled into that), and preventative meds like flea/tick treatment ($10-30 monthly). Treats and toys add another $10-20 a month if you're not going crazy. So realistically, you're looking at $150-250 per month for a medium dog all-in during normal years, more if something comes up health-wise. Pet insurance is something to think about too—it runs $15-50 monthly depending on coverage, and it can be a lifesaver if your dog needs surgery or has chronic issues. Some people find it worth it, others don't. Since you're working from home you'll probably have lower dog-sitting costs too, which is a money saver if you travel occasionally. Does that fit with your budget?

That's totally doable. I make decent money and I love animals, so I'm willing to invest in this. Now I'm actually getting excited. When do you think I should make my first shelter visit? Should I wait until I have all the supplies bought first, or what's the order of operations?

Honestly, I'd do a scouting trip to the shelter first without the pressure of supplies ready. Go look around, meet some dogs, talk to staff, get a feel for the place and what's available. This helps you figure out what you actually want versus what you think you want. Once you find a dog you're interested in, some shelters require a waiting period or application process anyway, so you'll have time to order supplies. Don't overthink it—most adoption timelines are 3-7 days from first visit to bringing the dog home. My suggestion: call Metro this week, visit next week if possible, and once you've identified a real candidate, then order your supplies knowing exactly what size dog you're getting and any special needs they might have. That way you're not stuck with a small collar that doesn't fit. Plus, the shelter can give you info on what food they've been feeding the dog, which matters for transitioning to your own brand. Sound good?

Perfect, I like that approach. I'll call them tomorrow and introduce myself. One more thing though—I'm a little nervous about the adjustment period. Like, how long does it typically take for a dog to settle in and feel comfortable at home?

The adjustment period is real but usually quicker than people think. The first 24-48 hours your dog will likely be quiet, maybe not eating much—this is normal because everything is new and overwhelming. By day 3-4, they usually start relaxing and showing their actual personality. Most dogs are pretty settled within 2-3 weeks once they understand the routine. Here's what helps: keep the first week low-key, maintain a consistent schedule with meals and walks, use that crate or bed as a safe space they can retreat to, and don't bombard them with visitors right away. The 'honeymoon period' is real—many dogs are perfect angels for the first few weeks, then you see their real personality quirks emerge around week 4-6. That's when you figure out if the match is working or if you need to adjust training, which is also normal. Just be patient and consistent. Dogs are resilient and they adapt way better than we think. The fact that you're working from home is actually ideal because you'll be around during this transition.

That's reassuring. I hadn't thought about the timeline that way. So like, should I expect the dog to have accidents in the house even if they're housetrained at the shelter, or will they mostly just hold it?

Yeah, it's pretty common for even housetrained dogs to have an accident or two in the first week because they're stressed and don't know where to go yet. The key is establishing a routine fast: take them out first thing when you wake up, after meals, after playtime, and before bed. Most dogs will catch on within a few days once they understand the pattern. The fact that you work from home is huge here—you can take them out frequently and prevent accidents rather than dealing with them. Get enzymatic cleaner for any accidents that do happen because the smell can make them want to pee in that spot again. If they're having frequent accidents after two weeks, it could signal a UTI or other issue worth checking with a vet. But honestly, with your schedule and patience, you'll likely be fine. Dogs are generally eager to be good, they just need clear communication about where the bathroom is. Have you thought about where you'll set up their bed and crate area?
I have a decent sized living room with a corner I was thinking of using. Is that a good spot, or should the crate be somewhere different? Also, how big should the crate actually be?

Living room corner is perfect—dogs like to be near their people but also have their own space. Just avoid putting it right by the front door (too stimulating) or directly in a cold draft. The crate size is important: it should be big enough for your dog to stand up, turn around, and lie down comfortably, but not so huge that they can pee in one corner and sleep in another. Too large and they won't feel that den-like security. Measure your dog once you know which one you're getting, then get an appropriately-sized crate. If you get a smaller dog like a Corgi or French Bulldog, probably a medium crate (around 30-36 inches). Medium dogs might need a large or extra-large. Pro tip: get a crate with a divider if you're unsure of sizing, so you can adjust it as they grow (though with an adult dog this doesn't matter). Make the crate inviting by putting a comfortable bed inside and feeding them meals there sometimes. It becomes a safe space, not a punishment. Does that help clarify it?

Yeah, that's super helpful. I'm starting to feel pretty prepared now. But I just want to double-check one thing—in New York apartments, are there any rules or restrictions about dogs I should know about? Like, do I need to register them or get special licenses?

Good thinking. In NYC, dogs do need to be registered with the city and have a rabies vaccination—it's required by law. The registration is pretty straightforward and usually around $8-13 per year for a regular dog, or free if your dog is spayed/neutered. Your vet will likely give you the rabies vaccine and paperwork, then you register through the NYC Department of Health. Most apartments have their own pet policies too—some allow dogs with deposits, some have size or breed restrictions. Definitely check your lease before committing to adoption because the last thing you want is a conflict with your landlord. Some buildings require a pet deposit (usually $300-1000) or add a pet fee to your monthly rent. Since Metro Animal Rescue is in NYC, they'll probably ask about your housing situation during the adoption process anyway. They're pretty thorough about making sure the situation works. Have you checked your lease yet, or do you know if your building allows dogs?

I'm in a larger building with a management company and honestly I'm not 100% sure about the pet policy. I should check that before I get too committed to this idea. That would be really awkward if I fell in love with a dog and then found out I couldn't keep them.

Absolutely right call—that's the smart thing to do first. Reach out to your building's management office and ask about their pet policy clearly: do they allow dogs, are there breed/size restrictions, is there a deposit or monthly fee, and do you need prior approval? Get it in writing if possible. Most buildings in NYC are pretty accommodating about dogs these days, but some still have restrictions. Once you confirm that's not an issue, then you can move forward with the shelter visit with total peace of mind. It's actually great that you're being thorough about this—it shows you're taking the responsibility seriously. Check that off your list this week, and then next week you can confidently call Metropolitan Animal Rescue and start the process. You're doing this the right way.
